en.extract:
The olive-capped coua (Coua olivaceiceps) is a species of cuckoo in the family Cuculidae.
It is endemic to southwest Madagascar.
The olive-capped coua was formally described in 1873 by the English ornithologist Richard Bowdler Sharpe under the binomial name Sericosomus olivaceiceps. Sharpe specified the type locality as southwestern Madagascar. He had been sent a specimen by the French naturalist Alfred Grandidier. The olive-capped coua is now one of ten species placed in the genus Coua, all of which are endemic to Madagascar. The specific epithet olivaceiceps combines the Modern Latin olivaceus meaning "olive-coloured" with Latin -ceps meaning "capped".
The olive-capped coua was formerly treated as a subspecies of the red-capped coua (Coua ruficeps). It was elevated to species status based on differences in plumage and vocalisation.

The '''olive-capped coua''' ('''''Coua olivaceiceps''''') is a species of [[cuckoo]] in the family [[Cuculidae]].
It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to southwest [[Madagascar]].

The olive-capped coua was [[species description|formally described]] in 1873 by the English ornithologist [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe]] under the [[binomial name]] ''Sericosomus olivaceiceps''. Sharpe specified the [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] as southwestern Madagascar. He had been sent a specimen by the French naturalist [[Alfred Grandidier]].<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Sharpe | first=Richard Bowdler | author-link=Richard Bowdler Sharpe | date=1873 | title=On the Cuculidae of the Ethiopian region | journal=
Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London | volume= | pages=578–624 [615] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/28504890}}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1940 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=4 | publisher=Harvard University Press | location=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=66 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14476537 }}</ref> The olive-capped coua is now one of ten species placed in the genus ''[[Coua]]'', all of which are endemic to Madagascar.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=August 2024 | title=Turacos, bustards, cuckoos, mesites, sandgrouse | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/turacos/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=30 August 2024}}</ref> The specific epithet ''olivaceiceps'' combines the [[Modern Latin]] ''olivaceus'' meaning "olive-coloured" with [[Latin]] ''-ceps'' meaning "capped".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=olivaceiceps | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=olivaceiceps | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=30 August 2024 }}</ref>

The olive-capped coua was formerly treated as a [[subspecies]] of the [[red-capped coua]] (''Coua ruficeps''). It was elevated to species status based on differences in plumage and vocalisation.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Dowsett-Lemaire | first=F. | date=2004 | title=Review: Oiseaux de Madagascar, Mayotte, Comoros, Seychelles, Réunion, Mauritius (Huguet & Chappuis 2003) | journal=Bulletin of the African Bird Club | volume=11 | issue=2 | pages=161–164 [161] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/51745550 }}</ref><ref name=ioc/>
